title: Aversive learning hijacks a brain sugar sensor to consolidate memory
methods: GAL4/UAS binary expression with TARGET system (tub-GAL80ts)
data_sources: FAFB full adult fly brain EM connectome (FlyWire)
Davie et al. 2018 single-cell transcriptomics (~60k fly brain cells; SCope)
Behavioral T-maze performance indices (source data provided with paper)
In vivo two-photon imaging (unprocessed images available on request)
FlyPad feeding assay data (source data provided with paper)

data_scope.organism: Drosophila melanogaster, young adult (<5 days), mixed-sex for behavior
data_scope.behavioral_n: 8-17 performance indices per condition (each derived from 2 groups of 20-50 flies)
data_scope.imaging_n: 7-21 flies per condition (female only for surgery)
data_scope.connectome: Full adult fly brain (FAFB); 89 FB.5/6-to-dFB synapses identified
data_scope.transcriptomics: ~60,000 adult fly brain cells (Davie et al. Cell 2018)
data_scope.time_resolution: Calcium imaging: one frame per 385 ms; pyruvate/glucose imaging: 1-2 frames/s over 7-16 min
data_scope.data_availability: No mandatory public deposition; source data and unprocessed images available on request; FAFB and SCope publicly available

core_conclusion: Spaced repetition of aversive olfactory learning in Drosophila hijacks the brain's internal fructose sensor (Gr43a neurons) by inducing a fasted-like state through cholinergic disinhibition of upstream dFB neurons via FB.5/6 neurons and mAchR-B. Post-training sugar intake activates the disinhibited Gr43a neurons, which release thyrostimulin (Gpa2-Gpb5) to trigger metabolic activation of mushroom-body alpha/beta neurons via their Lgr1 receptor, consolidating long-term memory. The same mechanism operates in appetitive learning, establishing thyrostimulin as a universal consolidation signal. Spaced training also drives non-homeostatic sugar appetite, providing a neural circuit basis for emotional eating.
why_it_matters: Reframes the spacing effect as a metabolic-cognitive AND gate; reveals a circuit mechanism for non-homeostatic hunger akin to emotional eating; identifies thyrostimulin as a shared consolidation hormone for appetitive and aversive LTM; provides a pharmacological handle to reduce the number of training sessions needed for LTM.
why_nature: Cross-disciplinary integration of circuit genetics, metabolic imaging, EM connectomics, and transcriptomics; first complete circuit-to-behavior explanation of the spacing effect; unexpected co-option of a feeding sensor by non-food-related experience; broad translational relevance to human cognitive and metabolic health.
added_value_to_my_research: AND-gate consolidation logic -> dual-condition activation thresholds in R2SFCA catchment modeling
Multi-timescale circuit cascade -> hierarchical spatiotemporal GNN separating fast vs. slow public-health signals
Connectome + transcriptomics -> gateway node discovery combining urban network structure and mobility flows
Metabolic state modulation of Gr43a sensitivity -> chronic disease prevalence as spatial covariate in mental health accessibility models
Spacing-function / reset-window framework -> temporal gating in spatiotemporal KDE and GNN models for stable mobility-based health prediction

figures.caption: Food intake after spaced training activates brain Gr43a neurons to allow LTM formation — silencing/activation experiments define the critical post-training consolidation window and show spaced training restores fructose sensitivity in satiated flies

Spaced training disinhibits fructose-sensing neurons despite satiety — dFB calcium activity is suppressed after spaced training via cholinergic FB.5/6 neurons acting through mAchR-B; FAFB connectome shows 89 FB.5/6-to-dFB synapses

Thyrostimulin (Gpa2-Gpb5) from fructose-sensing neurons targets mushroom-body alpha/beta neurons via Lgr1 — knockdowns abolish LTM and pyruvate/glucose metabolic activation of Kenyon cells within 2 h of spaced training

Brain fructose-sensing neurons mediate fasted-like feeding behaviour after spaced training — FlyPad assay shows strong sucrose preference post-training, dependent on the Gr43a-thyrostimulin-Lgr1 signalling axis

Disinhibition of brain fructose-sensing neurons facilitates aversive LTM — blocking TkR99D in Gr43a neurons or silencing dFB neurons reduces the spacing requirement from 5x to 2x training sessions, bypassing the spacing effect
